text: the sub-total colectomy by way of the paroscopic for severe RCH.
Four trocars are placed, with two positioned at the sites designated for future ileostomies and colostomies during the laparoscopic colectomy procedure.
The surgeon visually examines the transverse colon, noting its inflamed tubular appearance, indicative of ulcerative colitis resistant to medical treatment.
The surgeon begins the release of the right colon, starting from the cecum and progressing towards the right end of the colic, using an outside-to-inside approach.

After freeing the right colon, the surgeon creates a small mesenteric breach in front of the ileocecal valve to facilitate the introduction of the endogea for the section of the terminal ileum near the cecum.
The surgeon completes the sectioning of the last ileal segment and proceeds to dissect the mesoappendix, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins the sectioning of the right mesocolon, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the mesocolon tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects at the contact of the colon to preserve the ileocolic vessels, ensuring these structures remain intact during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the incision of the mesocolon, progressing from the lower section upwards towards the right colic angle.
The surgeon reaches the right colic angle, preparing to continue the dissection or sectioning process in this area.
The surgeon uses a surgical instrument positioned near the left clavicle to section the hepatocolic ligament, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the sectioning of the right mesocolon, facilitating the lowering and detachment of the thoracic angiocollis.
The surgeon proceeds to section the gastrocolic ligament, preparing for a homotectomy by carefully separating the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon sections the ligamo gastrocolic from right to left, stopping at the level of the left colic angle, using precise dissection techniques.
We then change the installation, the surgeon and his assistant are placed to the right of the patient who is installed in a rolling position.
The surgeon continues the sectioning of the left mesocolon, carefully blocking contact with the colon to facilitate the procedure.
The surgeon continues the maneuver to detach and lower the thoracic angiocollis, progressing towards the left colic angle.
The surgeon releases the left colon by performing a dissection from the bottom up and from the outside in, sectioning the left side of the mesocolon.
The surgeon continues releasing the tissue until reaching the left colic angle, where the colloparietal attachments are being sectioned.
The surgeon carefully sections the lenocolic ligament, a delicate step in the procedure due to the risk of bleeding, with visible attention to minimizing vascular injury.
The surgeon completes the sectioning of the ligament, resulting in the full detachment of the left colic angle from surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues the sectioning of the left mesocolon, proceeding from top to bottom, carefully detaching the mesocolon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon approaches the sigmoid mesocolon, performing a section away from the sigmoid colon by cutting one or two proximal sigmoid arteries.
The surgeon sections the sigmoid mesocolon, moving towards the top of the sigmoid petal, resulting in a well-released and tensionless colostomy.
The surgeon completes the extraction of the freed tissue through the incision in the left colon, ensuring all attachments are released.
The surgeon places the colostomy and proceeds to create the ileostomy, ensuring the bowel is not twisted during the process. The surgical intervention is completed.
